revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tajihi clan]][[ja:多治比氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11430839]] Wikidata name: Tajihi clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Tajihi clan |surname nihongo = 多治比氏 |image = |caption = |province = Kawachi (河内国丹比郡) |parent house = [[:en:Imperial Household of Japan|Imperial family]] |founder = [[Prince Tajihiko]] (多治比古王), grandson of [[Prince Kamueha]] (上殖葉王), son of [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]] |founding year = |cadet branches = }}
The '''Tajihi clan''' (多治比氏 ''Tajihi-uji''; also written as 多治, 丹比, 丹治, 丹墀) was a Japanese noble family. They were most prominent in the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], when they furnished several senior officers of the central government, before they were gradually driven out of the centre of power by the rival [[Fujiwara clan]].
== Name == The name of the Tajihi clan can be written with various combinations of [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]], including 多治比, 多治, 丹比, 丹治, and 丹墀.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The clan were descendants of [[Prince Tajihiko]],{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} who, according to tradition, was named for the ''tajihi'' (多治比, [[:en:Reynoutria japonica|Japanese knotweed]]) blossoms that were blown by at the time of his birth,{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} but it seems more likely that the clan were named for Tajihi District (丹比郡) in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== History == The Tajihi clan's ancestor Prince Tajihiko was a great-grandson of the sixth-century [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]] through his son [[Prince Kamueha]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Their imperial ancestry categorizes them as one of the ''kōbetsu'' (皇別) clans, descended from the [[:en:Japanese imperial family|Japanese imperial family]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The clan's original ''[[kabane]]'' was ''Kimi'' (公),{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} but were given the title ''[[:en:Mahito|Mahito]]'' (or ''Mabito'') by [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] in 684.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
{{illm|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served at the courts of Emperor Tenmu, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]] and [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]], rose to the prestigious Rank of [[:en:Sadaijin|Minister of the Left]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} His sons Ikemori (多治比池守), Agatamori (多治比県守), Hironari (多治比広成), Hirotari (多治比広足) held various key positions at court, including ''[[:en:Dainagon|Dainagon]]'' and ''[[:en:Chūnagon|Chūnagon]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} [[Tajihi no Mamune]] (多治比真宗, 769–823), a great-granddaughter of Ikemori, served as a consort of [[Emperor Kanmu]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
Until the middle of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] the Tajihi were one of the clans at the pinnacle of court society, but after this time became increasingly overshadowed by the [[Fujiwara clan]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Around the start of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] the Tajihi clan mostly disappeared from the court society.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The {{illm|Tantō clan|ja|丹党}}, a group of warriors in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] and one of the so-called {{illm|Musashi Shichitō|ja|武蔵七党}}, were remote descendants of the Tajihi clan who took up martial ways.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== References == {{reflist|colwidth=20em}}
== Works cited == * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = [[Encyclopedia Nipponica]] |last = Mayuzumi |first = Hiromichi |author-link = Hiromichi Mayuzumi |title = Tajihi-uji |language = japanese |year = 1994 |publisher = [[Shogakukan]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/多治比氏-93251#E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E5.85.A8.E6.9B.B8.28.E3.83.8B.E3.83.83.E3.83.9D.E3.83.8B.E3.82.AB.29 |accessdate = 2017-11-25 }}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]]
}}
[[en:Tajihi clan]]

== References == {{reflist|colwidth=20em}}
== Works cited == * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = [[Encyclopedia Nipponica]] |last = Mayuzumi |first = Hiromichi |author-link = Hiromichi Mayuzumi |title = Tajihi-uji |language = japanese |year = 1994 |publisher = [[Shogakukan]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/多治比氏-93251#E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E5.85.A8.E6.9B.B8.28.E3.83.8B.E3.83.83.E3.83.9D.E3.83.8B.E3.82.AB.29 |accessdate = 2017-11-25 }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tajihi clan]][[ja:多治比氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430839]] Wikidata name: Tajihi clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Tajihi clan |surname nihongo = 多治比氏 |image = |caption = |province = Kawachi (河内国丹比郡) |parent house = [[:en:Imperial Household of Japan|Imperial family]] |founder = [[Prince Tajihiko]] (多治比古王), grandson of [[Prince Kamueha]] (上殖葉王), son of [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]] |founding year = |cadet branches = }}
The '''Tajihi clan''' (多治比氏 ''Tajihi-uji''; also written as 多治, 丹比, 丹治, 丹墀) was a Japanese noble family. They were most prominent in the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], when they furnished several senior officers of the central government, before they were gradually driven out of the centre of power by the rival [[Fujiwara clan]].
== Name == The name of the Tajihi clan can be written with various combinations of [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]], including 多治比, 多治, 丹比, 丹治, and 丹墀.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The clan were descendants of [[Prince Tajihiko]],{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} who, according to tradition, was named for the ''tajihi'' (多治比, [[:en:Reynoutria japonica|Japanese knotweed]]) blossoms that were blown by at the time of his birth,{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} but it seems more likely that the clan were named for Tajihi District (丹比郡) in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== History == The Tajihi clan's ancestor Prince Tajihiko was a great-grandson of the sixth-century [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]] through his son [[Prince Kamueha]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Their imperial ancestry categorizes them as one of the ''kōbetsu'' (皇別) clans, descended from the [[:en:Japanese imperial family|Japanese imperial family]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The clan's original ''[[kabane]]'' was ''Kimi'' (公),{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} but were given the title ''[[:en:Mahito|Mahito]]'' (or ''Mabito'') by [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] in 684.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
{{illm|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served at the courts of Emperor Tenmu, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]] and [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]], rose to the prestigious Rank of [[:en:Sadaijin|Minister of the Left]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} His sons Ikemori (多治比池守), Agatamori (多治比県守), Hironari (多治比広成), Hirotari (多治比広足) held various key positions at court, including ''[[:en:Dainagon|Dainagon]]'' and ''[[:en:Chūnagon|Chūnagon]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} [[Tajihi no Mamune]] (多治比真宗, 769–823), a great-granddaughter of Ikemori, served as a consort of [[Emperor Kanmu]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
Until the middle of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] the Tajihi were one of the clans at the pinnacle of court society, but after this time became increasingly overshadowed by the [[Fujiwara clan]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Around the start of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] the Tajihi clan mostly disappeared from the court society.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The {{illm|Tantō clan|ja|丹党}}, a group of warriors in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] and one of the so-called {{illm|Musashi Shichitō|ja|武蔵七党}}, were remote descendants of the Tajihi clan who took up martial ways.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== References == {{reflist|colwidth=20em}}
== Works cited == * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = [[Encyclopedia Nipponica]] |last = Mayuzumi |first = Hiromichi |author-link = Hiromichi Mayuzumi |title = Tajihi-uji |language = japanese |year = 1994 |publisher = [[Shogakukan]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/多治比氏-93251#E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E5.85.A8.E6.9B.B8.28.E3.83.8B.E3.83.83.E3.83.9D.E3.83.8B.E3.82.AB.29 |accessdate = 2017-11-25 }}

{{illm|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served at the courts of Emperor Tenmu, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]] and [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]], rose to the prestigious Rank of [[:en:Sadaijin|Minister of the Left]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} His sons Ikemori (多治比池守), Agatamori (多治比県守), Hironari (多治比広成), Hirotari (多治比広足) held various key positions at court, including ''[[:en:Dainagon|Dainagon]]'' and ''[[:en:Chūnagon|Chūnagon]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} [[Tajihi no Mamune]] (多治比真宗, 769–823), a great-granddaughter of Ikemori, served as a consort of [[Emperor Kanmu]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
Until the middle of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] the Tajihi were one of the clans at the pinnacle of court society, but after this time became increasingly overshadowed by the [[Fujiwara clan]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Around the start of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] the Tajihi clan mostly disappeared from the court society.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The {{illm|Tantō clan|ja|丹党}}, a group of warriors in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] and one of the so-called {{illm|Musashi Shichitō|ja|武蔵七党}}, were remote descendants of the Tajihi clan who took up martial ways.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tajihi clan]][[ja:多治比氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430839]] Wikidata name: Tajihi clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Tajihi clan |surname nihongo = 多治比氏 |image = |caption = |province = Kawachi (河内国丹比郡) |parent house = [[:en:Imperial Household of Japan|Imperial family]] |founder = [[Prince Tajihiko]] (多治比古王), grandson of [[Prince Kamueha]] (上殖葉王), son of [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]] |founding year = |cadet branches = }}
The '''Tajihi clan''' (多治比氏 ''Tajihi-uji''; also written as 多治, 丹比, 丹治, 丹墀) was a Japanese noble family. They were most prominent in the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], when they furnished several senior officers of the central government, before they were gradually driven out of the centre of power by the rival [[Fujiwara clan]].
== Name == The name of the Tajihi clan can be written with various combinations of [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]], including 多治比, 多治, 丹比, 丹治, and 丹墀.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The clan were descendants of [[Prince Tajihiko]],{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} who, according to tradition, was named for the ''tajihi'' (多治比, [[:en:Reynoutria japonica|Japanese knotweed]]) blossoms that were blown by at the time of his birth,{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} but it seems more likely that the clan were named for Tajihi District (丹比郡) in [[:en:Kawachi Province|Kawachi Province]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== History == The Tajihi clan's ancestor Prince Tajihiko was a great-grandson of the sixth-century [[:en:Emperor Senka|Emperor Senka]] through his son [[Prince Kamueha]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Their imperial ancestry categorizes them as one of the ''kōbetsu'' (皇別) clans, descended from the [[:en:Japanese imperial family|Japanese imperial family]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The clan's original ''[[kabane]]'' was ''Kimi'' (公),{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} but were given the title ''[[:en:Mahito|Mahito]]'' (or ''Mabito'') by [[:en:Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu]] in 684.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
{{illm|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served at the courts of Emperor Tenmu, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]] and [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]], rose to the prestigious Rank of [[:en:Sadaijin|Minister of the Left]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} His sons Ikemori (多治比池守), Agatamori (多治比県守), Hironari (多治比広成), Hirotari (多治比広足) held various key positions at court, including ''[[:en:Dainagon|Dainagon]]'' and ''[[:en:Chūnagon|Chūnagon]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} [[Tajihi no Mamune]] (多治比真宗, 769–823), a great-granddaughter of Ikemori, served as a consort of [[Emperor Kanmu]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
Until the middle of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] the Tajihi were one of the clans at the pinnacle of court society, but after this time became increasingly overshadowed by the [[Fujiwara clan]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Around the start of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] the Tajihi clan mostly disappeared from the court society.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The {{illm|Tantō clan|ja|丹党}}, a group of warriors in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] and one of the so-called {{illm|Musashi Shichitō|ja|武蔵七党}}, were remote descendants of the Tajihi clan who took up martial ways.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== References == {{reflist|colwidth=20em}}
== Works cited == * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = [[Encyclopedia Nipponica]] |last = Mayuzumi |first = Hiromichi |author-link = Hiromichi Mayuzumi |title = Tajihi-uji |language = japanese |year = 1994 |publisher = [[Shogakukan]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/多治比氏-93251#E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E5.85.A8.E6.9B.B8.28.E3.83.8B.E3.83.83.E3.83.9D.E3.83.8B.E3.82.AB.29 |accessdate = 2017-11-25 }}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese clans]]
}}

{{illm|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served at the courts of Emperor Tenmu, [[:en:Empress Jitō|Empress Jitō]] and [[:en:Emperor Monmu|Emperor Monmu]], rose to the prestigious Rank of [[:en:Sadaijin|Minister of the Left]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} His sons Ikemori (多治比池守), Agatamori (多治比県守), Hironari (多治比広成), Hirotari (多治比広足) held various key positions at court, including ''[[:en:Dainagon|Dainagon]]'' and ''[[:en:Chūnagon|Chūnagon]]''.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} [[Tajihi no Mamune]] (多治比真宗, 769–823), a great-granddaughter of Ikemori, served as a consort of [[Emperor Kanmu]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
Until the middle of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] the Tajihi were one of the clans at the pinnacle of court society, but after this time became increasingly overshadowed by the [[Fujiwara clan]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Around the start of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] the Tajihi clan mostly disappeared from the court society.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The {{illm|Tantō clan|ja|丹党}}, a group of warriors in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] and one of the so-called {{illm|Musashi Shichitō|ja|武蔵七党}}, were remote descendants of the Tajihi clan who took up martial ways.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== References == {{reflist|colwidth=20em}}

They were most prominent in the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], when they furnished several senior officers of the central government, before they were gradually driven out of the centre of power by the rival [[Fujiwara clan]].

Until the middle of the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] the Tajihi were one of the clans at the pinnacle of court society, but after this time became increasingly overshadowed by the [[Fujiwara clan]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Around the start of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] the Tajihi clan mostly disappeared from the court society.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The {{illm|Tantō clan|ja|丹党}}, a group of warriors in [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]] and one of the so-called {{illm|Musashi Shichitō|ja|武蔵七党}}, were remote descendants of the Tajihi clan who took up martial ways.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}

They were most prominent in the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, when they furnished several senior officers of the central government, before they were gradually driven out of the centre of power by the rival [[Fujiwara clan]].

{{illm|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served at the courts of Emperor Tenmu, {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|WD=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō}} and {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu}}, rose to the prestigious Rank of [[:en:Sadaijin|Minister of the Left]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} His sons Ikemori (多治比池守), Agatamori (多治比県守), Hironari (多治比広成), Hirotari (多治比広足) held various key positions at court, including ''{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}'' and ''{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon}}''.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} [[Tajihi no Mamune]] (多治比真宗, 769–823), a great-granddaughter of Ikemori, served as a consort of [[Emperor Kanmu]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
Until the middle of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} the Tajihi were one of the clans at the pinnacle of court society, but after this time became increasingly overshadowed by the [[Fujiwara clan]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Around the start of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} the Tajihi clan mostly disappeared from the court society.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The {{illm|Tantō clan|ja|丹党}}, a group of warriors in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}} and one of the so-called {{illm|Musashi Shichitō|ja|武蔵七党}}, were remote descendants of the Tajihi clan who took up martial ways.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tajihi clan]][[ja:多治比氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tajihi clan]][[ja:多治比氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tajihi clan]][[ja:多治比氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430839]] Wikidata name: Tajihi clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Tajihi clan |surname nihongo = 多治比氏 |image = |caption = |province = Kawachi (河内国丹比郡) |parent house = [[:en:Imperial Household of Japan|Imperial family]] |founder = [[Prince Tajihiko]] (多治比古王), grandson of [[Prince Kamueha]] (上殖葉王), son of {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}} |founding year = |cadet branches = }}
The '''Tajihi clan''' (多治比氏 ''Tajihi-uji''; also written as 多治, 丹比, 丹治, 丹墀) was a Japanese noble family. They were most prominent in the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, when they furnished several senior officers of the central government, before they were gradually driven out of the centre of power by the rival [[Fujiwara clan]].
== Name == The name of the Tajihi clan can be written with various combinations of {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}}, including 多治比, 多治, 丹比, 丹治, and 丹墀.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The clan were descendants of [[Prince Tajihiko]],{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} who, according to tradition, was named for the ''tajihi'' (多治比, {{ill|Reynoutria japonica|en|Reynoutria japonica|WD=Q18421053|lt=Japanese knotweed}}) blossoms that were blown by at the time of his birth,{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} but it seems more likely that the clan were named for Tajihi District (丹比郡) in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== History == The Tajihi clan's ancestor Prince Tajihiko was a great-grandson of the sixth-century {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}} through his son [[Prince Kamueha]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Their imperial ancestry categorizes them as one of the ''kōbetsu'' (皇別) clans, descended from the [[:en:Japanese imperial family|Japanese imperial family]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The clan's original ''[[kabane]]'' was ''Kimi'' (公),{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} but were given the title ''{{ill|Mahito|en|Mahito|WD=Q85846522|lt=Mahito}}'' (or ''Mabito'') by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu}} in 684.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
{{illm|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served at the courts of Emperor Tenmu, {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|WD=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō}} and {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu}}, rose to the prestigious Rank of [[:en:Sadaijin|Minister of the Left]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} His sons Ikemori (多治比池守), Agatamori (多治比県守), Hironari (多治比広成), Hirotari (多治比広足) held various key positions at court, including ''{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}'' and ''{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon}}''.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} [[Tajihi no Mamune]] (多治比真宗, 769–823), a great-granddaughter of Ikemori, served as a consort of [[Emperor Kanmu]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
Until the middle of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} the Tajihi were one of the clans at the pinnacle of court society, but after this time became increasingly overshadowed by the [[Fujiwara clan]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Around the start of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} the Tajihi clan mostly disappeared from the court society.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The {{illm|Tantō clan|ja|丹党}}, a group of warriors in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}} and one of the so-called {{illm|Musashi Shichitō|ja|武蔵七党}}, were remote descendants of the Tajihi clan who took up martial ways.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== References == {{reflist|colwidth=20em}}
== Works cited == * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = [[Encyclopedia Nipponica]] |last = Mayuzumi |first = Hiromichi |author-link = Hiromichi Mayuzumi |title = Tajihi-uji |language = japanese |year = 1994 |publisher = [[Shogakukan]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/多治比氏-93251#E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E5.85.A8.E6.9B.B8.28.E3.83.8B.E3.83.83.E3.83.9D.E3.83.8B.E3.82.AB.29 |accessdate = 2017-11-25 }}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tajihi clan]][[ja:多治比氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tajihi clan]][[ja:多治比氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tajihi clan]][[ja:多治比氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11430839]] Wikidata name: Tajihi clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:43, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Tajihi clan |surname nihongo = 多治比氏 |image = |caption = |province = Kawachi (河内国丹比郡) |parent house = [[:en:Imperial Household of Japan|Imperial family]] |founder = [[Prince Tajihiko]] (多治比古王), grandson of [[Prince Kamueha]] (上殖葉王), son of {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}} |founding year = |cadet branches = }}
The '''Tajihi clan''' (多治比氏 ''Tajihi-uji''; also written as 多治, 丹比, 丹治, 丹墀) was a Japanese noble family. They were most prominent in the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, when they furnished several senior officers of the central government, before they were gradually driven out of the centre of power by the rival [[Fujiwara clan]].
== Name == The name of the Tajihi clan can be written with various combinations of {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji}}, including 多治比, 多治, 丹比, 丹治, and 丹墀.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The clan were descendants of [[Prince Tajihiko]],{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} who, according to tradition, was named for the ''tajihi'' (多治比, {{ill|Reynoutria japonica|en|Reynoutria japonica|WD=Q18421053|lt=Japanese knotweed}}) blossoms that were blown by at the time of his birth,{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} but it seems more likely that the clan were named for Tajihi District (丹比郡) in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== History == The Tajihi clan's ancestor Prince Tajihiko was a great-grandson of the sixth-century {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka}} through his son [[Prince Kamueha]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Their imperial ancestry categorizes them as one of the ''kōbetsu'' (皇別) clans, descended from the [[:en:Japanese imperial family|Japanese imperial family]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The clan's original ''[[kabane]]'' was ''Kimi'' (公),{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} but were given the title ''{{ill|Mahito|en|Mahito|WD=Q85846522|lt=Mahito}}'' (or ''Mabito'') by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu}} in 684.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
{{illm|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served at the courts of Emperor Tenmu, {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|WD=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō}} and {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu}}, rose to the prestigious Rank of [[:en:Sadaijin|Minister of the Left]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} His sons Ikemori (多治比池守), Agatamori (多治比県守), Hironari (多治比広成), Hirotari (多治比広足) held various key positions at court, including ''{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}'' and ''{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon}}''.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} [[Tajihi no Mamune]] (多治比真宗, 769–823), a great-granddaughter of Ikemori, served as a consort of [[Emperor Kanmu]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
Until the middle of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}} the Tajihi were one of the clans at the pinnacle of court society, but after this time became increasingly overshadowed by the [[Fujiwara clan]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Around the start of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} the Tajihi clan mostly disappeared from the court society.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The {{illm|Tantō clan|ja|丹党}}, a group of warriors in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}} and one of the so-called {{illm|Musashi Shichitō|ja|武蔵七党}}, were remote descendants of the Tajihi clan who took up martial ways.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== References == {{reflist|colwidth=20em}}
== Works cited == * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = [[Encyclopedia Nipponica]] |last = Mayuzumi |first = Hiromichi |author-link = Hiromichi Mayuzumi |title = Tajihi-uji |language = japanese |year = 1994 |publisher = [[Shogakukan]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/多治比氏-93251#E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E5.85.A8.E6.9B.B8.28.E3.83.8B.E3.83.83.E3.83.9D.E3.83.8B.E3.82.AB.29 |accessdate = 2017-11-25 }}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tajihi clan]][[ja:多治比氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tajihi clan]][[ja:多治比氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Tajihi clan]][[ja:多治比氏]]
{{short description|Japanese noble family}} {{Japanese clan name| |surname = Tajihi clan |surname nihongo = 多治比氏 |image = |caption = |province = Kawachi (河内国丹比郡) |parent house = [[:en:Imperial Household of Japan|Imperial family]] |founder = [[Prince Tajihiko]] (多治比古王), grandson of [[Prince Kamueha]] (上殖葉王), son of {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka|1=Emperor Senka}} |founding year = |cadet branches = }}
The '''Tajihi clan''' (多治比氏 ''Tajihi-uji''; also written as 多治, 丹比, 丹治, 丹墀) was a Japanese noble family. They were most prominent in the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, when they furnished several senior officers of the central government, before they were gradually driven out of the centre of power by the rival [[Fujiwara clan]].
== Name == The name of the Tajihi clan can be written with various combinations of {{ill|Kanji|en|Kanji|WD=Q82772|lt=Kanji|1=Kanji}}, including 多治比, 多治, 丹比, 丹治, and 丹墀.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The clan were descendants of [[Prince Tajihiko]],{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} who, according to tradition, was named for the ''tajihi'' (多治比, {{ill|Reynoutria japonica|en|Reynoutria japonica|WD=Q18421053|lt=Japanese knotweed|1=Reynoutria japonica}}) blossoms that were blown by at the time of his birth,{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} but it seems more likely that the clan were named for Tajihi District (丹比郡) in {{ill|Kawachi Province|en|Kawachi Province|WD=Q1047150|lt=Kawachi Province|1=Kawachi Province}}.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== History == The Tajihi clan's ancestor Prince Tajihiko was a great-grandson of the sixth-century {{ill|Emperor Senka|en|Emperor Senka|WD=Q329728|lt=Emperor Senka|1=Emperor Senka}} through his son [[Prince Kamueha]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Their imperial ancestry categorizes them as one of the ''kōbetsu'' (皇別) clans, descended from the [[:en:Japanese imperial family|Japanese imperial family]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The clan's original ''[[kabane]]'' was ''Kimi'' (公),{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} but were given the title ''{{ill|Mahito|en|Mahito|WD=Q85846522|lt=Mahito|1=Mahito}}'' (or ''Mabito'') by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|en|Emperor Tenmu|WD=Q335181|lt=Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} in 684.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
{{illm|Tajihi no Shima|ja|多治比嶋}}, who served at the courts of Emperor Tenmu, {{ill|Empress Jitō|en|Empress Jitō|WD=Q232026|lt=Empress Jitō|1=Empress Jitō}} and {{ill|Emperor Monmu|en|Emperor Monmu|WD=Q357246|lt=Emperor Monmu|1=Emperor Monmu}}, rose to the prestigious Rank of [[:en:Sadaijin|Minister of the Left]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} His sons Ikemori (多治比池守), Agatamori (多治比県守), Hironari (多治比広成), Hirotari (多治比広足) held various key positions at court, including ''{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon|1=Dainagon}}'' and ''{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon|1=Chūnagon}}''.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} [[Tajihi no Mamune]] (多治比真宗, 769–823), a great-granddaughter of Ikemori, served as a consort of [[Emperor Kanmu]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
Until the middle of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} the Tajihi were one of the clans at the pinnacle of court society, but after this time became increasingly overshadowed by the [[Fujiwara clan]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Around the start of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} the Tajihi clan mostly disappeared from the court society.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The {{illm|Tantō clan|ja|丹党}}, a group of warriors in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} and one of the so-called {{illm|Musashi Shichitō|ja|武蔵七党}}, were remote descendants of the Tajihi clan who took up martial ways.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
== References == {{reflist|colwidth=20em}}
== Works cited == * {{cite encyclopedia |encyclopedia = [[Encyclopedia Nipponica]] |last = Mayuzumi |first = Hiromichi |author-link = Hiromichi Mayuzumi |title = Tajihi-uji |language = japanese |year = 1994 |publisher = [[Shogakukan]] |url = https://kotobank.jp/word/多治比氏-93251#E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E5.85.A8.E6.9B.B8.28.E3.83.8B.E3.83.83.E3.83.9D.E3.83.8B.E3.82.AB.29 |accessdate = 2017-11-25 }}

Until the middle of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} the Tajihi were one of the clans at the pinnacle of court society, but after this time became increasingly overshadowed by the [[Fujiwara clan]].{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} Around the start of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} the Tajihi clan mostly disappeared from the court society.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}} The {{illm|Tantō clan|ja|丹党}}, a group of warriors in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}} and one of the so-called {{illm|Musashi Shichitō|ja|武蔵七党}}, were remote descendants of the Tajihi clan who took up martial ways.{{sfnm|1a1=Mayuzumi|1y=1994}}
